title: BUG: segfault from `random_raw` from instance initialized from invalid value
author: devdanzin
body:
### Describe the issue:

It's possible to segfault the interpreter by (re)initializing a random generator with invalid data, then calling `random_raw`.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy.random

p = numpy.random.PCG64DXSM()

try:
    p.__init__(("",))
except ValueError:
    pass
p.random_raw()
```

### Error message:

```shell
Thread 1 "python" received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
```

```
2.3.0.dev0+git20250415.e151f0d
3.13.3+ experimental free-threading build (heads/3.13:83cb89b941b, Apr 18 2025, 20:59:43) [Clang 19.1.7 (++20250114103253+cd708029e0b2-1exp120250114103309.40)]
```

```

{'architecture': 'SkylakeX',

'internal_api': 'openblas',
'num_threads': 16,
'prefix': 'libscipy_openblas',
'threading_layer': 'pthreads',

```

### Context for the issue:

I have been fuzzing Numpy using [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil) by @vstinner. I realize these crashes are unlikely to be triggered in normal usage and therefore might be of low priority.

The fuzzing was done with an ASAN free-threading clang build and not confirmed on a GILfull non-sanitizer GCC build yet.

Ping @rkern because this is happening inside the Cython RNG code.

comments.author: rkern
comments.body:
`BitGenerator.__init__()` (re)sets the state to a null pointer before trying to process the arguments. When you instantiate the object and then (re)run the `__init__()` with an invalid argument, the state gets (re)set but the exception gets in the way. In typical usage, this exception won't be a problem because one typically instantiates and runs the `__init__()` in the same expression before the user gets a hold of the instance, so they never get a chance to call methods on the invalid instance. But in this circumstance of rerunning `__init__()`, you already have a reference to the instance.

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/main/numpy/random/bit_generator.pyx#L525

Another, slightly more realistic, reproducer would be to call `__new__()` then `__init__()` separately.

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> bg = np.random.PCG64.__new__(np.random.PCG64)
>>> bg.random_raw()
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[3], line 1
----> 1 bg.random_raw()
File bit_generator.pyx:663, in numpy.random.bit_generator.BitGenerator.random_raw()
File _common.pyx:95, in numpy.random._common.random_raw()
AttributeError: __exit__
>>> bg.lock is None
True
>>> bg.__init__(("",))
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[7], line 1
----> 1 bg.__init__(("",))
File _pcg64.pyx:123, in numpy.random._pcg64.PCG64.__init__()
File bit_generator.pyx:535, in numpy.random.bit_generator.BitGenerator.__init__()
File bit_generator.pyx:315, in numpy.random.bit_generator.SeedSequence.__init__()
File bit_generator.pyx:389, in numpy.random.bit_generator.SeedSequence.get_assembled_entropy()
File bit_generator.pyx:148, in numpy.random.bit_generator._coerce_to_uint32_array()
File bit_generator.pyx:138, in numpy.random.bit_generator._coerce_to_uint32_array()
ValueError: unrecognized seed string
>>> bg.lock is None
False
>>> bg.random_raw()
[1]    2766765 segmentation fault (core dumped)  ipython --profile doctest
```

From the first example, we can see that we can prevent _most_ of the badness if we also make sure to reset the `lock` attribute back to `None` if the rest of the `__init__()` fails (e.g. with a `try: except Exception: self.lock = None; raise`. Access to the state should be protected by the lock everywhere, so keeping it `None` when we think it is likely to be invalid will cause graceful(ish) exceptions instead of segmentation faults. This probably only needs to be done in `BitGenerator.__init__()`, but `Philox.__init__()` might also need a similar treatment in case its handling of its `key` argument fails.
